title: Law of Conservation of Energy
definition: Energy cannot be created or destroyed within an isolated system; it can only transform from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant over time.

proofs.description: Noether's theorem establishes conservation of energy as a consequence of time-translation symmetry in physical laws
proofs.reference: Noether, E. (1918). Invariante Variationsprobleme. Nachrichten von der Gesellschaft der Wissenschaften zu Göttingen, Mathematisch-Physikalische Klasse, 1918, 235-257.

proofs.description: Countless experiments across physics, chemistry, and engineering have validated energy conservation to high precision
proofs.reference: Joule, J.P. (1850). On the Mechanical Equivalent of Heat. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, 140, 61-82.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Energy can change forms (like from motion to heat) but the total amount always stays the same. You can't create energy from nothing or make it disappear.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: The total energy of any isolated system remains constant. Energy transforms between kinetic, potential, thermal, chemical, and other forms, but the sum is invariant.
difficulty_levels.expert: Energy conservation emerges from time-translation symmetry via Noether's theorem. In general relativity, energy conservation requires careful formulation due to curved spacetime, but in flat spacetime and local inertial frames, it holds exactly.

extra_fields.historical_context: The concept evolved through the work of Leibniz, Joule, Helmholtz, and Mayer in the 1840s-1850s, culminating in the first law of thermodynamics. Noether's theorem (1918) provided the deep mathematical foundation linking conservation laws to symmetries.
extra_fields.limitations: Energy conservation in general relativity is subtle; global energy conservation breaks down in expanding spacetime (cosmology). Virtual particles in quantum field theory temporarily violate conservation but average out over time.
extra_fields.discoverer: Julius Robert von Mayer, James Prescott Joule, Hermann von Helmholtz
